---
title: "JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity"
date: 2026-06-04
description: "An R package that estimates stochastic production functions while correcting for crop-choice selection bias, following Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Returns a clean interpretation of which inputs drive output variance and which are biased by selection."
image: "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/robcareta/JPselect/main/man/figures/README-risk-plot.png"
categories:
  - R
  - Package
  - Production functions
  - Heckman correction
  - Just-Pope
  - Agricultural economics
---

<p class="back-nav"><a href="../index.qmd">← Back to Projects</a></p>

## Overview

`JPselect` is an R package that implements the three-step estimation procedure of [Koundouri & Nauges (2005)](https://www.jstor.org/stable/40987295) for [Just-Pope](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0304407678900068) (1978, 1979) stochastic production functions with sample-selection bias from crop choice. The package bundles the probit selection equation, the mean production function with an Inverse Mills Ratio, the variance (risk) function, and a full-pipeline bootstrap, behind a single high-level interface.

Version 0.2.0 adds functional-form flexibility: three mean-function forms (linear-quadratic, quadratic, Cobb-Douglas), two risk-function forms (Cobb-Douglas, exponential), a translog guard, and a side-by-side comparison helper `jp_compare()` for sensitivity analysis across specifications.

::: {.callout-note}
Code, documentation, and a runnable example are available on [GitHub](https://github.com/robcareta/JPselect). Install with `remotes::install_github("robcareta/JPselect")`.
:::

## The problem it solves

When farmers choose what to grow, that choice depends on characteristics (soil quality, water access, farming experience) that also affect productivity. Estimating a production function on the chosen sub-sample, for example just the vegetable growers, mixes the technological relationship between inputs and output with the self-selection of farms into crops. The result is **biased** estimates of how inputs affect both mean output **and** its variability.

Koundouri and Nauges' main finding is that **skipping the Heckman correction can flip the sign or kill the significance** of risk-function coefficients. An applied researcher who reports the naive estimates can therefore reach the wrong conclusion about which inputs are risk-increasing or risk-decreasing.

`JPselect` runs both the corrected and uncorrected specifications and shows them side by side, so the bias is visible directly.

## What's inside

The package exposes a small surface area:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `jp_fit()` | One-call wrapper for the full three-step pipeline plus the with-vs-without comparison. Accepts `mean_form` and `risk_form` arguments. Returns a `jpfit` S3 object. |
| `print(fit)` | Risk-function table plus a plain-language interpretation: which inputs are risk-increasing or decreasing, where selectivity correction changes the conclusion, whether the Mill's ratio is significant. |
| `summary(fit)` | All three stages of the procedure end-to-end: probit, mean function with Mill's ratio, with vs. without risk function. |
| `plot(fit)` | Headline coefficient plot of the risk function. |
| `jp_compare()` | Fits `jp_fit()` under every combination of supplied mean and risk forms, returns a `jpcompare` object with summary diagnostics and a wide side-by-side coefficient table. |
| `jp_export()` | Writes the four resulting tables to Excel (one workbook, five sheets), LaTeX (booktabs tables for a paper), or CSV (one file per table). |
| `simulate_kiti_data()` | Synthetic 239-farm dataset that mimics the paper's Cyprus sample, for demonstration and testing. |

Individual stage functions (`estimate_selection`, `estimate_mean_function`, `estimate_risk_function`) are also exported for users who want finer control.

## Sample output

`print(fit)` ends with a structured interpretation block:

```
At p < 0.10, with selectivity correction:
  Risk-DECREASING inputs : (none)
  Risk-INCREASING inputs : (none)

Does selectivity correction change the conclusion?
  labor : significant without correction (p=0.022) but NOT significant once corrected (p=0.116)
  water : significant without correction (p=0.030) but NOT significant once corrected (p=0.519)

Selection-bias test (Mill's ratio in the mean function):
  coef = +0.527, p = 0.036 -- selection bias DETECTED.
  Prefer the 'with correction' column above.
```

The headline plot shows each input twice (red = with selectivity correction, teal = without). When the two estimates disagree, the selectivity bias is visible at a glance:

![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/robcareta/JPselect/main/man/figures/README-risk-plot.png){fig-alt="Risk-function coefficients with vs. without Heckman correction" width="100%"}

## Functional form flexibility

Functional form is a research-design choice, not a property of the data. Version 0.2.0 lets the user pick from three mean functions and two risk functions and compare them side by side.

**Mean function** (`mean_form` argument of `jp_fit()`):

- `"linear_quadratic"` (default): the Koundouri-Nauges specification, with linear, quadratic, and pairwise interaction terms in inputs.
- `"quadratic"`: linear and quadratic terms only, no pairwise interactions. Useful when the sample is small relative to the input count.
- `"cobb_douglas"`: log-log specification. Shankar & Nelson (1999) show that the Cobb-Douglas mean paired with the Cobb-Douglas variance is robust to input endogeneity within the Just-Pope framework.

**Risk function** (`risk_form` argument of `jp_fit()`):

- `"cobb_douglas"` (default): the canonical Koundouri-Nauges form. Coefficients are variance elasticities. Requires strictly positive inputs.
- `"exponential"`: $h(x) = \exp(\xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j x_j)$. Coefficients are variance semi-elasticities. Handles zero-valued inputs (Saha, Havenner & Talpaz 1997; Tveterås 1999, 2000).

**Translog is deliberately blocked** at the API level. It implies a multiplicative interaction between the mean and variance functions, which breaks the additive identification structure that Just-Pope requires (Koundouri & Nauges 2005, footnote 5). Passing `mean_form = "translog"` raises an informative error pointing to the footnote.

**Sensitivity comparison.** `jp_compare()` fits the package across every combination of mean and risk forms supplied and returns a `jpcompare` object whose `print()` method produces a publication-ready table:

```
======================================================================
JPselect: comparison of functional-form specifications
======================================================================
6 combinations fitted on n = 95 selected farms.

SUMMARY (Step 1 + Step 2 diagnostics)
 Combo  Mean_form        Risk_form    Mean_R2 IMR_coef IMR_p Bias_at_10
 LQ/CD  linear_quadratic cobb_douglas 0.964   +0.527   0.036 YES
 Q/CD   quadratic        cobb_douglas 0.961   +0.371   0.105 no
 CD/CD  cobb_douglas     cobb_douglas 0.426   +0.706   0.360 no
 LQ/Exp linear_quadratic exponential  0.964   +0.527   0.036 YES
 Q/Exp  quadratic        exponential  0.961   +0.371   0.105 no
 CD/Exp cobb_douglas     exponential  0.426   +0.706   0.360 no

RISK FUNCTION COEFFICIENTS (with selectivity correction)
 Input       LQ/CD    Q/CD     CD/CD    LQ/Exp  Q/Exp    CD/Exp
 fertilizers +0.057   +0.131** +0.036   +0.013  +0.150** +0.064
 pesticides  +0.007   +0.111** +0.053   -0.081  +0.061   +0.067
 labor       -0.107** -0.069   -0.111   -0.129  -0.187** -0.004
 water       -0.046   -0.045   +0.434** -0.115* -0.070   +0.122***
```

The wide coefficient table makes it easy to spot inputs whose sign or significance depends on the specification, exactly the kind of robustness check a referee will want.

## Quick example

```r
library(JPselect)

farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 42)

# Single specification (default linear-quadratic + Cobb-Douglas risk)
fit <- jp_fit(
  data                 = farms,
  selection_var        = "vegetables",
  selection_covariates = c("rainfall", "irrigated", "dist_town",
                           "dist_coast", "experience"),
  output_var           = "revenue",
  input_vars           = c("fertilizers", "pesticides", "labor", "water"),
  shifter_vars         = c("machinery", "rainfall", "irrigated",
                           "dist_town", "dist_coast", "experience"),
  bootstrap_reps       = 500
)

print(fit)
plot(fit)
jp_export(fit, "results.xlsx")

# Sensitivity comparison across all 6 form combinations
cmp <- jp_compare(
  data                 = farms,
  selection_var        = "vegetables",
  selection_covariates = c("rainfall", "irrigated", "dist_town",
                           "dist_coast", "experience"),
  output_var           = "revenue",
  input_vars           = c("fertilizers", "pesticides", "labor", "water"),
  shifter_vars         = c("machinery", "rainfall", "irrigated",
                           "dist_town", "dist_coast", "experience"),
  bootstrap_reps       = 0,
  mean_forms           = c("linear_quadratic", "quadratic", "cobb_douglas"),
  risk_forms           = c("cobb_douglas", "exponential")
)

print(cmp)
```

## Technical details

| | |
|---|---|
| **Language** | R (100%) |
| **Key dependencies** | `stats`, `sandwich`, `ggplot2`, `openxlsx` (for Excel export) |
| **Standard errors** | 500-replication nonparametric bootstrap that resamples the full pipeline (probit, IMR, mean function, residuals, risk function) on every replication. |
| **License** | MIT |
| **Repository** | [github.com/robcareta/JPselect](https://github.com/robcareta/JPselect) |
| **Installation** | `remotes::install_github("robcareta/JPselect")` |
| **Status** | v0.2.0 (functional-form flexibility and sensitivity comparison) |

## How to cite

If you use `JPselect` in published work, please cite the package together with the underlying paper.

**Package:**

> Cardenas Retamal, R. (2026). *JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity Correction.* R package version 0.2.0. [https://github.com/robcareta/JPselect](https://github.com/robcareta/JPselect)

**Underlying paper:**

> Koundouri, P., & Nauges, C. (2005). On Production Function Estimation with Selectivity and Risk Considerations. *Journal of Agricultural and Resource Economics*, 30(3), 597-608.

In R, the canonical citations are also available via:

```r
citation("JPselect")
```

## References

Heckman, J. (1979). Sample selection bias as a specification error. *Econometrica*, 47, 153-161. [https://doi.org/10.2307/1912352](https://doi.org/10.2307/1912352)

Just, R. E., & Pope, R. D. (1978). Stochastic representation of production functions and econometric implications. *Journal of Econometrics*, 7(1), 67-86. [https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-4076(78)90006-4](https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-4076(78)90006-4)

Koundouri, P., & Nauges, C. (2005). On Production Function Estimation with Selectivity and Risk Considerations. *Journal of Agricultural and Resource Economics*, 30(3), 597-608. [https://www.jstor.org/stable/40987295](https://www.jstor.org/stable/40987295)

Saha, A., Havenner, A., & Talpaz, H. (1997). Stochastic production function estimation: small sample properties of ML versus FGLS. *Applied Economics*, 29(4), 459-469.

Shankar, B., & Nelson, C. H. (1999). Joint risk preference-technology estimation with a primal system: comment. *American Journal of Agricultural Economics*, 81(1), 241-244.

Tveterås, R. (1999). Production risk and productivity growth: some findings for Norwegian salmon aquaculture. *Journal of Productivity Analysis*, 12(2), 161-179.

Tveterås, R. (2000). Flexible panel data models for risky production technologies with an application to salmon aquaculture. *Econometric Reviews*, 19(3), 367-389.
